sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the red path clearly. The red path (R) covers:
- Rows 9-12, cols 39-41: RRR (top-right)
- Rows 12-40, col 40: R (right side vertical)
- Rows 38-42, cols 14-40: RRRRR (bottom horizontal)
The red path forms a "C" shape or a backwards "E" shape. It covers the right and bottom parts of the maze but not the left/middle parts.
Looking at the white corridors:
- Rows 14-18, cols 20-24: white corridor (not covered by red)
- Rows 14-18, cols 32-38: white corridor (partially covered by red at col 40)
- Rows 26-37, cols 20-38: white corridor (not covered by red)
- Rows 43-47, cols 20-38: white corridor (not covered by red)
The red path hasn't covered the left/middle corridors. The goal is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49 (blue square).
Wait, looking at the red path more carefully:
- Row 38: BRRRRRB (cols 14-18 are R)
- Row 39: BBRRRRB (cols 15-18 are R)
- Row 40: BRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRB (cols 14-40 are R)
- Row 41: BBRRRRB (cols 15-18 are R)
- Row 42: BRRRRRB (cols 14-18 are R)
So the red path covers rows 38-42, cols 14-40. This is the bottom horizontal section.
The red path also covers rows 9-12, cols 39-41 (top-right) and rows 12-40, col 40 (right vertical).
The goal is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49. The red path needs to extend to reach the goal. But the red path currently ends at row 42, col 14 (bottom-left of the covered area).
Wait, I think I'm misunderstanding the game. Let me reconsider.
The red path seems to be a "trail" that the player creates by pressing SPACE at different positions. The player is at the top corridor (rows 8-12). When the player presses SPACE at a certain horizontal position, the red path extends from that position downward through the maze.
Looking at the current red path:
- The top-right section (rows 9-12, cols 39-41) was created when the player was at cols 38-42 and pressed SPACE.
- The right vertical section (rows 12-40, col 40) extends downward from the top-right section.
- The bottom horizontal section (rows 38-42, cols 14-40) extends leftward from the right vertical section.
So the red path traces a route from the top-right to the bottom-left of the maze. The goal is at the bottom-right. The red path needs to continue to reach the goal.
But the player can only move left/right at the top corridor. How does the player affect the red path in other parts of the maze?
Actually, maybe the game is simpler than I thought. The player moves left/right at the top, and pressing SPACE "drops" the red path from the current position. The red path follows the white corridors in the maze. The goal is to have the red path reach the blue square at the bottom.
Looking at the current state:
- The player is at cols 14-18 (start position).
- The red path covers the right and bottom sections of the maze.
- The goal is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49.
The red path hasn't reached the goal yet. The red path ends at row 42, cols 14-18 (bottom-left). The goal is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49 (bottom-right).
Wait, looking at the maze structure, the white corridor at rows 43-47, cols 20-38 connects to the goal area at rows 48-55, cols 43-49. The red path needs to extend through this corridor to reach the goal.
But the red path currently ends at row 42, cols 14-18. To reach the goal, the red path needs to extend:
1. From row 42, cols 14-18 down to rows 43-47, cols 20-38
2. From rows 43-47, cols 20-38 to rows 48-55, cols 43-49 (goal)
But the player is at the top corridor. How does the player affect the red path in the bottom corridor?
Maybe the game is about the player "activating" different sections of the maze by pressing SPACE at different horizontal positions. Each press of SPACE extends the red path from the current position through the maze.
Let me try pressing SPACE at the current position (cols 14-18) to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at start (cols 14-18). Try SPACE here to see if red path extends differently
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
